---
title: "week8"
author: "Iyenoma Goodnews Osabuohien"
date: "2026-03-17"
output:
  word_document: default
  html_document: default
---



```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```



```{R}
contentdatas <- read.csv("../data/contentdatas.csv")
library(dplyr)
library(corrplot)
```



```{r cars}

plot(contentdatas)
```



```{r}
summary(contentdatas)

```




```{r}
#check_model(model_r)
# very useless

df1 <- contentdatas
cor(df1)
corrplot(cor(df1), method="number")
corrplot(cor(df1), method="color")
corrplot(cor(df1), method="circle")
corrplot(cor(df1), method="square")
```


## Multiple regression

```{r}
model_r2 <- lm(Y ~ X1 + X2, data = contentdatas)

summary(model_r2)

```

```{r}
summary(model_r2)$coefficients

```


```{r}

round(coef(model_r2), 3)

```

```{r}

summary(model_r2)$coefficients[, "Pr(>|t|)"]
round(summary(model_r2)$coefficients[, "Pr(>|t|)"], 3)


```







```{r}
summary(model_r2)

```






## R Markdown






This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document. You can embed an R code chunk like this:






## Including Plots

You can also embed plots, for example:

```{r pressure, echo=FALSE}
plot(pressure)
```

Note that the `echo = FALSE` parameter was added to the code chunk to prevent printing of the R code that generated the plot.
